summary: 4th-century BC Athenian playwright, foremost representative of New Comedy. Only Dyskolos survives nearly complete. Other fragmentary plays include The Girl from Samos, Perikeiromene, and The Shield. Aristophanes of Byzantium asked of him and life, 'which of you imitated the other?'

works.name: Dyskolos (The Grouch)
works.description: Comedy about the curmudgeonly Knemon, whose rescue from a well leads him to accept Sostratos as a son-in-law. Only near-complete surviving play, found on a Bodmer papyrus.

clues.clue: Knemon is a grouchy old man whose daughter marries Sostratos after Sostratos/Gorgias saves Knemon from falling in a well
examples: the title character's rescue from a well leads him to accept the love-struck Sostratos as a son-in-law

clues.clue: Prologue recited by the god Pan, whose worshippers make up the chorus; Pan causes Sostratos to fall in love
examples: A prologue recited by the god Pan opens a play by this author

clues.clue: Only near-complete text found on a Bodmer papyrus in Egypt
examples: The only near-complete text of Menander's Dyskolos was found on one of these ancient Egyptian manuscripts named for the Swiss scholar

works.name: The Girl from Samos (Samia)
works.description: Play about misunderstandings between Chrysis, Demeas, and others, ending in the marriage of Moschion and Plangon.

clues.clue: Demeas and lover Chrysis; confusion over baby nursing threatens wedding of Plangon and Moschion
examples: confusion over one character nursing a baby assumed to be hers threatens to derail the wedding between Plangon and Moschion

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Facts about Menander and New Comedy.

clues.clue: Foremost representative of Athenian New Comedy; contrasted with Aristophanes's Old Comedy
examples: Menander is the foremost representative of the New Comedy

clues.clue: Aristophanes of Byzantium: 'which of you imitated the other?' about Menander and life
examples: led Aristophanes of Byzantium to ask of this author and life, 'which of you imitated the other?'

clues.clue: Originated maxims: 'He whom the gods love dies young' and 'Bad company corrupts good character'
examples: Saint Paul's phrase 'Bad company corrupts good character' as well as Caesar's 'the die is cast' are said to be derived from this author

clues.clue: Names a villa in Pompeii with a fresco of him reading
examples: This man names a villa in Pompeii containing a fresco of him seated reading

clues.clue: Influenced Moliere; works rediscovered in Egypt
examples: This author of Girl from Samos and Perikeiromene influenced Moliere

clues.clue: Sappho legend of jumping off a rock after rejection by Phaon first appeared in a play by this author
examples: A recurring Greek literary tale in which Sappho jumps off a rock into the sea after being rejected by Phaon first appeared in a play by this author

comprehensive_summary:
Menander (c. 342-290 BC) was an Athenian playwright and the foremost representative of New Comedy, which focused on everyday domestic life in contrast to the political satire of Aristophanes's Old Comedy. Aristophanes of Byzantium famously asked of Menander and life, 'which of you imitated the other?', reflecting his posthumous reputation as a realist.

His only near-complete surviving play is Dyskolos (The Grouch), found on a Bodmer papyrus in Egypt. It opens with a prologue by the god Pan, who causes Sostratos to fall in love with the daughter of the curmudgeonly Knemon. After Sostratos or Gorgias saves Knemon from falling into a well, Knemon agrees to accept Sostratos as a son-in-law.

The Girl from Samos (Samia) involves misunderstandings between Demeas, his lover Chrysis, and others over a baby, ending in the marriage of Moschion and Plangon. Other fragmentary works include Perikeiromene (a mercenary cuts off his wife's hair) and The Shield.

Menander originated famous maxims including 'He whom the gods love dies young' and 'Bad company corrupts good character' (later quoted by Saint Paul). He names a villa in Pompeii with a fresco of him reading. His works influenced Moliere and Roman comedians.
